title: Slow Internet Speed
description: Troubleshooting slow internet connection or poor network speed.

steps.title: Run a Speed Test
steps.instruction:
*⚡ Step 1: Run a Speed Test*
• Open a browser and go to `https://www.speedtest.net` or `https://fast.com`.
• Click *Go* and wait for the results.
• Note your Download and Upload speed.
_Expected: Speeds should match your subscribed internet plan. If significantly lower, proceed with next steps._

steps.title: Restart Your Router
steps.instruction:
*🔌 Step 2: Restart Your Router*
• Unplug your WiFi router from the power socket.
• Wait 30 seconds, then plug it back in.
• Wait 2 minutes for the router to fully restart.
• Run the speed test again to compare.
_Expected: Speed improves after router restart._

steps.title: Move Closer to Router or Use Ethernet
steps.instruction:
*📶 Step 3: Move Closer to Router or Use Ethernet*
• WiFi signal weakens with distance and walls. Try moving closer to the router.
• If possible, connect directly using a *LAN/Ethernet cable* for the fastest speeds.
_Expected: Wired connection or closer position improves speed significantly._

steps.title: Close Bandwidth-Heavy Applications
steps.instruction:
*🚫 Step 4: Close Bandwidth-Heavy Applications*
• Check if any apps are downloading updates in the background (Windows Update, OneDrive, Google Drive, Dropbox).
• Press *Ctrl + Shift + Esc* → Task Manager → *Network* column — end any process using heavy bandwidth.
• Pause Windows Update via *Settings → Windows Update → Pause Updates*.
_Expected: Other apps stop consuming bandwidth, freeing up speed._

steps.title: Flush DNS Cache
steps.instruction:
*🛠️ Step 5: Flush DNS Cache*
• Ask IT to run the DNS flush for you, or:
• Press *Windows + R* → type `cmd` → click OK.
• In the window, type: `ipconfig /flushdns` and press Enter.
• You should see: *Successfully flushed the DNS Resolver Cache*.
• Restart your browser and test again.
_Expected: Pages load faster after DNS cache is cleared._
